name: Communalism and Partition

sourceRefs.label: 2024_q page 35 Q53 (Muslim League founder, with answer key)
2024_q page 27 Q36 (Lahore/Pakistan Resolution date, with answer key)
2022_q1 page 56 Q179-180 (Partition of Bengal 1905 facts; Second Round Table Conference, with answer key)
Direct Action Day, Mountbatten Plan, Radcliffe Line, Partition casualties/displacement — standard well-established history
nextias.com, Wikipedia - Simon Commission ('Simon Go Back'), Nehru Report/Jinnah's Fourteen Points

researchNotes: 9th topic of Unit III. 3 real hits across 2022/2024 spanning both the 1905 Bengal Partition and the 1940s lead-up to 1947 Partition. Rounded out with standard Partition-of-India facts (Direct Action Day, Mountbatten Plan, Radcliffe Line) since no direct past-paper hit was found for those specific 1947-era events. Expanded 2026-07 pass: added the RSS's 1925 founding (a distinct communal-organization fact from the Muslim League's 1906 founding already covered), the Cabinet Mission Plan and Interim Government of 1946 (the failed last attempt at a united India before Partition became inevitable), the Two-Nation Theory's intellectual lineage (Sir Syed Ahmad Khan's early groundwork through to Jinnah's explicit 1940 formulation), why Punjab/Bengal's partition was especially fraught, and the striking fact that the Radcliffe Line was announced only after independence day itself. Round-2 pass (2026-07, second expansion): added the Simon Commission (1927)/'Simon Go Back' protest and the Nehru Report (1928)/Jinnah's Fourteen Points (1929) — an important earlier link in the chain from constitutional-reform disputes to the widening Congress-League rift, filling the gap between the 1905 Bengal Partition material and the already-covered 1930s-40s events. Roughly a third more mock questions (13→17).

reading.intro: This topic spans TWO partitions — the 1905 Partition of Bengal (later reversed) and the 1947 Partition of India (permanent) — plus the communal organizations/events that bridge them. Real exam evidence favors specific founding dates and named resolutions.

qa.question: Real exam: Who founded the All-India Muslim League, in 1906?
qa.answer: Khwaja Salimullah (along with other founders) — not Muhammad Ali Jinnah, who became its most prominent leader LATER, not at its founding.

qa.question: Real exam: Evaluate — (i) the 1905 Partition of Bengal was the most striking example of British divide-and-rule policy (ii) at a 1905 Calcutta meeting, Surendranath Banerjee called for a boycott of British goods and institutions (iii) the Swadeshi movement was formally proclaimed at a Calcutta Town Hall meeting on 7 August 1905.
Real exam: When was the Pakistan Resolution (Lahore Resolution) passed by the All India Muslim League, and where?
What was 'Direct Action Day', and what did it lead to?
qa.answer: 16 August 1946 — a Muslim League call for mass demonstrations, which spiralled into large-scale communal riots in Bengal and elsewhere, remembered as the 'Great Calcutta Killing'.

qa.question: What was the Mountbatten Plan, and when was it announced?
qa.answer: Announced 3 June 1947 (the '3rd June Plan') by Lord Mountbatten, the last Viceroy — the proposal to partition British India into the two independent dominions of India and Pakistan.

qa.question: Who drew the boundary line between India and Pakistan, and what is it called?
qa.answer: Sir Cyril Radcliffe — hence the 'Radcliffe Line', criticized for its rushed, arbitrary demarcation that left millions on the 'wrong' side of the new border.

qa.question: What was the human scale of Partition's impact?
qa.answer: Death toll estimates range from 200,000 to 2,000,000; roughly 15 million Hindus, Sikhs, and Muslims were displaced crossing the hastily drawn borders — one of history's largest mass migrations.

qa.question: When and by whom was the Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh (RSS) founded?
qa.answer: Founded 27 September 1925 by K.B. Hedgewar in Nagpur, aiming to unite the Hindu community through character training and discipline — a distinct communal organization from the Muslim League (1906).

qa.question: What was the Cabinet Mission Plan (1946)?
qa.answer: A British proposal to transfer power to a united India, with provincial autonomy/grouping for Muslim-majority areas rather than outright partition. It ultimately failed once the Muslim League refused to settle for anything short of a fully separate Pakistan.

qa.question: What was the Interim Government (formed September 1946)?
qa.answer: A transitional government formed under the Cabinet Mission process, initially staffed only by Congress representatives (the Muslim League refused to join at first) — it governed British India through its final pre-independence months.

qa.question: What is the Two-Nation Theory, and who is most associated with it?
qa.answer: The idea that Hindus and Muslims form two distinct nations with separate religious, cultural, and social identities, requiring separate homelands. Sir Syed Ahmad Khan laid early intellectual groundwork; Muhammad Ali Jinnah championed it explicitly from 1940 onward as the basis for demanding Pakistan.

qa.question: Why was partitioning Punjab and Bengal especially difficult, compared to simply splitting India and Pakistan?
qa.answer: Both provinces had only slim Muslim majorities with highly mixed, heterogeneous populations — the Boundary Commission had to draw internal lines splitting the provinces themselves, inevitably leaving large communities on the 'wrong' side of the border wherever the line fell.

qa.question: When was the Radcliffe Line's exact boundary officially announced, relative to independence itself?
qa.answer: Only two days AFTER independence — 17 August 1947 (though the lines were finalized by 9-12 August) — meaning India and Pakistan became independent on 14-15 August 1947 without yet knowing their own final borders.
qa.tip: Striking exam fact: independence came BEFORE the actual border was announced, not after — a detail that surprises many test-takers.

qa.question: Round-2 pass — What was the Simon Commission (1927), and why was it so controversial in India?
qa.answer: A 7-member British Parliamentary commission (chaired by Sir John Simon), appointed in November 1927 to review constitutional/political reform in India. It provoked outrage across the political spectrum — Congress, the Muslim League, and other groups alike — because it contained NO Indian members at all. It was met with black-flag protests and the slogan 'Simon Go Back' (coined by activist Yusuf Meherally) upon its arrival in Bombay, 3 February 1928.

qa.question: What was the Nehru Report (1928), and how did Jinnah respond to it?
qa.answer: Drafted by Motilal Nehru in September 1928 (as an Indian-authored counter to the all-British Simon Commission), it proposed Dominion Status with full internal self-government and rejected separate communal electorates. Muhammad Ali Jinnah rejected it as a 'Hindu Document' and responded in 1929 with his own 'Fourteen Points', setting out the Muslim League's minimum demands (including continued separate electorates and reserved legislative seats) — a key milestone in the widening Congress-League rift that eventually fed into the Two-Nation Theory and Partition.

questions.prompt: The All-India Muslim League was founded in 1906 by:
questions.explanation: Khwaja Salimullah — Jinnah became prominent later, not at founding.

questions.prompt: The Swadeshi movement was formally proclaimed at a Calcutta Town Hall meeting on:
The Pakistan (Lahore) Resolution was passed by the Muslim League on:
'Direct Action Day' (16 August 1946) led to communal violence remembered as the:
The Mountbatten Plan, proposing the partition of British India, was announced on:
The boundary line between India and Pakistan, drawn by Sir Cyril Radcliffe, is known as the:
The Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh (RSS) was founded in 1925 by:
The Cabinet Mission Plan (1946) originally proposed:
options: A united India with provincial autonomy/grouping for Muslim-majority areas
Continued British rule for 10 more years
A federation with Nepal and Ceylon
questions.explanation: A united India with provincial autonomy, not outright partition.

questions.prompt: The Interim Government formed in September 1946 initially had representatives from only:
questions.explanation: The Congress (the Muslim League joined later).

questions.prompt: The Two-Nation Theory, explicitly championed by Jinnah from 1940, held that:
options: India should remain a single secular state
Hindus and Muslims are two distinct nations requiring separate homelands
Princely states should become fully independent
Only linguistic states should be created
questions.explanation: Hindus and Muslims are two distinct nations requiring separate homelands.

questions.prompt: Partitioning Punjab and Bengal was especially difficult mainly because:
options: They had no Muslim population at all
Both had only slim Muslim majorities with highly mixed populations
They refused to join either India or Pakistan
The British had no maps of these regions
questions.explanation: Both provinces had slim Muslim majorities with heterogeneous, mixed populations.

questions.prompt: The Radcliffe Line's exact boundary was officially announced:
questions.trick: Remember: independence came BEFORE the final border announcement, not after — a commonly missed sequencing detail.

questions.prompt: Sir Syed Ahmad Khan is associated with laying early intellectual groundwork for:
The Simon Commission (1927) provoked nationwide outrage across the political spectrum chiefly because:
options: It contained no Indian members at all
It proposed abolishing all provincial legislatures
It was funded entirely by princely states
questions.explanation: It was an all-British commission with zero Indian representation.

questions.prompt: The slogan "Simon Go Back", raised upon the Commission's arrival in Bombay (3 February 1928), is credited to:
questions.explanation: Yusuf Meherally, an independence activist and Congress Party member.

questions.prompt: The Nehru Report (1928), drafted by Motilal Nehru, proposed:
options: Dominion Status with full internal self-government, rejecting separate communal electorates
A federation under continued direct British rule
questions.explanation: Dominion Status with full internal self-government, and no separate communal electorates.

questions.prompt: Jinnah rejected the Nehru Report as a "Hindu Document" and responded in 1929 with:
questions.explanation: Jinnah's 'Fourteen Points', setting out the Muslim League's minimum demands.
